title: Taking OCaml and Eio for a spin
excerpt: My first blog post on this website was about Haskell. I've always been interested in functional programming but the complexity of Haskell never seemed worth.
content_html:
My first blog post on this website was about Haskell. I've always been interested in functional programming but the complexity of Haskell never seemed worth.

I have been vaguely aware of OCaml for a long time but never dipped my toes in. It has been having a bit of a renaissance recently with multicore support, effects and a more systems focussed spin called OxCaml from Jane Street. Now seems a great time to learn it and I was curious about Eio, a concurrency framework. It uses the aforementioned effects but more interesting to me is it is meant to be deterministic, which could be helpful for testing.

Books & Tutorials

The first thing to do is pick a tutorial or a book. A quick google search later I had found Real World OCaml. Assuming that it would be similar to Real World Haskell (which I enjoyed and was very hands on) and assuming I already had sufficient FP chops, I dived in.

Impatient to look at syntax and semantics I skipped over the more prosey Prologue. If I had read it I would have been told that the book chooses to use Jane Street's Base standard library replacement. It seems that historically the real standard library was quite anemic, only intended to cover the needs of the compiler. This is no longer true and it has slowly been growing. Still, it doesn't seem unreasonable to me that a book called Real World OCaml use the libraries from the biggest real world user of the language.

Anyway, I surely learnt my lesson to read things more carefully and went back to the beginning to start again. The first half of the book is well written and I happily followed along. I started to get lost around GADTs and first class modules though. I think the book lacks for exercises, so once something unfamilar comes up you don't have the full grasp of the basics to bail you out.

I went searching for something a bit easier and found CS3110 which has both videos and text to suit all tastes. It also had exercises which helped a lot. I would suggest to people that they start with this course and then graduate onto RWO for the more advanced features. I like RWO although if you are looking for a project based book like RWH, you'll be disappointed.

How It Feels In The Hand

Overall OCaml is very enjoyable to write code in. It has a nice mix of functional and imperative programming. For example, to loop through a list you are either using a recursive function or List.map and friends:

let print_recursive list =
  let rec loop i list =
    match list with
    | [] -> ()
    | x :: xs ->
      Printf.printf "[%d] => %i";
      loop (i+1) xs
  in
  loop 0 list

let print_fn = List.iteri (fun i x -> Printf.printf "[%d] => %i\n" i x)

let () =
  print_recursive ["foo"; "bar"; "baz"];
  print_fn ["foo"; "bar"; "baz"];

(*
[0] => foo
[1] => bar
[2] => baz
[0] => foo
[1] => bar
[2] => baz
*)

but you still have mutable variables and the like if you need them:

type client = { mutable request_id : int }

let dispatch cli ~msg =
  let request_id = cli.request_id in
  cli.request_id <- request_id + 1; (* update request_id *)
  
  Printf.printf "Dispatching %d/%s to server\n" request_id msg

let () =
  dispatch_cli ~msg:"Hello";
  dispatch_cli ~msg:"World"

(*
  => Dispatching 0/Hello to server
  => Dispatching 1/World to server
*)

I will say that the syntax feels quite wordy, e.g. the let .. in, match .. with constructs. I think syntax is the least interesting part of a programming language to me - I was happy even back in the days where Rust had sigils - so it doesn't bother me too much. Having named arguments (with the ~) is very nice.

The compiler bothers me more. When it encounters an error it seems to give up on the rest of the file. This makes the iteration loop quite slow - write code, get an error, fix the error, rebuild. There's very little chance to fix errors in bulk, especially if you're focussed on one module.

Eio

Eio is an effects-based IO concurrency library. It's relatively new but it has some interesting features such as io_uring support. Ultimately my goal is to write an implementation of the Raft consensus algorithm and try test it.

The Raft paper says to use remote procedure calls (RPCs) to communicate between the servers. A dumb RPC client/server seems like a great first project. Let's come up with a very simple protocol: a procedure call has a request id (int), a name (string) and a single argument (string). Integers are a single byte and a string is serialised as a byte for the length and then the data. As an example, to call the Echo procedure with Hello as request 10, you would send:

00000000: 0a04 4563 686f 0548 656c 6c6f            ..Echo.Hello

A response will just be the request id and the string return value.

Before we start, we need to familiarise ourselves with some jargon Eio uses for its building blocks:

  • Fiber - a thread of execution. Not a system thread though!
  • Switch - something that groups fibers together. A bit like a sync.WaitGroup in Go
  • Flow - something you can read or write to
  • Stream - a bounded, thread safe queue. Like a Go channel
  • Promise - I'm sure you know this one.

Starting with the server we will need to take a Flow and loop, reading procedure calls off it, invoking them and sending a response:

open Utils

type conn = {
  src : Eio.Buf_read.t;
  sink : Eio.Buf_write.t;
}

let rec conn_loop conn =
  let request_id = Eio.Buf_read.uint8 conn.src in

  let procedure_name = read_string conn.src in
  let body = read_string conn.src in
  
  traceln "rx %d/%s(%s)" request_id procedure_name body;

  (* In a real RPC system we might dispatch the invocation to a worker pool *)
  let res = match procedure_name with
  | "Echo" -> body
  | "Capitalise" -> String.capitalize_ascii body
  | _ -> failwith "unknown procedure"
  in

  traceln "tx %d/%s(%s) => %s" request_id procedure_name body res;

  Eio.Buf_write.uint8 conn.sink request_id;
  write_string conn.sink res;

  conn_loop conn

We define a type to hold a connection, as a server can have many of them. Note that we aren't using the flow directly here, instead we are using Buf_read or Buf_write types. That's so we can use buffered reading/writing and be more efficient. Operations on a Flow are done directly on the underlying resource (socket, file etc). Happily these buffered reader/writers have useful helper functions for reading/writing types which we use here to send/receive the request id.

read_string/write_string come from Utils:

let read_string src =
  let len = Eio.Buf_read.uint8 src in
  Eio.Buf_read.take len src
  
let write_string sink s =
  Eio.Buf_write.uint8 sink (String.length s);
  Eio.Buf_write.string sink s

Next we just need a helper to get the buffered flows and run the loop:

let handle_conn flow =
  let src = Eio.Buf_read.of_flow ~max_size:1024 flow in
  Eio.Buf_write.with_flow flow @@ fun sink ->
  conn_loop {src; sink}

and a test executable:

open Dumb_rpc
open Eio.Std

let port = 1470

let () =
  Eio_main.run @@ fun env ->
  Eio.Switch.run @@ fun sw ->
  let net = Eio.Stdenv.net env in

  let handle_client flow addr =
    traceln "Accepted connection from %a" Eio.Net.Sockaddr.pp addr;
    Dumb_rpc.Server.handle_conn ~sw flow
  in

  let addr = `Tcp (Eio.Net.Ipaddr.V4.loopback, port) in
  let socket = Eio.Net.listen net ~sw ~reuse_addr:true ~backlog:5 addr in
  Eio.Net.run_server socket handle_client
    ~on_error:(traceln "Error handling connection: %a" Fmt.exn)

and we're ready to test it:

> printf '\x00\x04Echo\x05Hello' | nc localhost 1470 -q0
Hello⏎
> printf '\x01\x0aCapitalise\x07matthew' | nc localhost 1470 -q0
Matthew⏎

Nice!

Client

For the client let's get a bit more fancy and make it safe to use concurrently. To do that we need to make sure that we are only writing a single procedure call to the socket at any one time. This will be done using two fibers: one for reading and one for writing. The writing one will take its work off a stream that we add to when a method is invoked:

type request = { procedure : string; arg : string; }

type t = {
  mutable request_id : int;
  writeq : request Eio.Stream.t; (* Read right to left: stream of request *)
}

let rec send_loop cli w =
  let req = Eio.Stream.take cli.writeq in
  let request_id = take_and_inc_request_id cli in
  write_request request;
  send_loop cli w

let invoke cli ~procedure ~arg =
  let req = {procedure; arg;} in
  Eio.Stream.add cli.writeq req;

But how do we get the response back to the caller? To do that we will send a promise on our stream as well and store it away in a hash table, with the key being the request id. This can be resolved later when the server has responded. Note that in Eio promises are actually split into two halves - the side that waits (Promise.t) and the side that resolves (Promise.u).

type waiter = { resolver : string Eio.Promise.u; }

type request = {
  procedure : string;
  arg : string;
  resolver : string Eio.Promise.u;
}

type t = {
  mutable request_id : int;

  writeq : request Eio.Stream.t;
  waiters : (int, waiter) Hashtbl.t;
}

let rec send_loop cli w =
  let req = Eio.Stream.take cli.writeq in
  let request_id = take_and_inc_request_id cli in
  Hashtbl.add cli.waiters request_id { resolver = req.resolver };
  
  traceln "tx %d/%s(%s)" request_id req.procedure req.arg;
  write_request request;

  send_loop cli w

let invoke cli ~procedure ~arg =
  let (promise, resolver) = Eio.Promise.create () in
  let req = {procedure; arg; resolver; } in
  Eio.Stream.add cli.writeq req;

  Eio.Promise.await promise

The receive loop can then just read responses and resolve promises:

let rec recv_loop cli r =
  let (request_id, body) = read_response
  
  traceln "rx %d => %s" request_id body;
  let waiter = Hashtbl.find cli.waiters request_id in
  Promise.resolve waiter.resolver body;
  
  recv_loop cli r

We can use it like so, from multiple different fibers:

open Eio.Std

let port = 1470

let () =
  Eio_main.run @@ fun env ->
  Eio.Switch.run @@ fun sw ->
  let net = Eio.Stdenv.net env in

  let addr = `Tcp (Eio.Net.Ipaddr.V4.loopback, port) in
  let flow = Eio.Net.connect ~sw net addr in

  let cli = Dumb_rpc.Client.create () in
  Eio.Fiber.fork_daemon ~sw (fun () -> Dumb_rpc.Client.run cli ~flow; `Stop_daemon);
  
  for i = 0 to 5 do
    Eio.Fiber.fork ~sw (fun () ->
      let arg = Printf.sprintf "arg-%d" i in
      traceln "Invoking Echo(%s)" arg;
      let res = Dumb_rpc.Client.invoke cli ~procedure:"Echo" ~arg in
      traceln "Got response Echo(%s) => %s" arg res
    )
  done

and see that it works:

> ./_build/default/bin/client.exe
+Invoking Echo(arg-0)
+Invoking Echo(arg-1)
+Invoking Echo(arg-2)
+Invoking Echo(arg-3)
+Invoking Echo(arg-4)
+Invoking Echo(arg-5)
+tx 0/Echo(arg-0)
+tx 1/Echo(arg-1)
+tx 2/Echo(arg-2)
+tx 3/Echo(arg-3)
+rx 0 => arg-0
+Got response Echo(arg-0) => arg-0
+tx 4/Echo(arg-4)
+rx 1 => arg-1
+Got response Echo(arg-1) => arg-1
+rx 2 => arg-2
+tx 5/Echo(arg-5)
+Got response Echo(arg-2) => arg-2
+rx 3 => arg-3
+rx 4 => arg-4
+Got response Echo(arg-3) => arg-3
+Got response Echo(arg-4) => arg-4
+rx 5 => arg-5
+Got response Echo(arg-5) => arg-5

Concluion

Overall OCaml has been fun to learn and easy to use. It sits at a number of sweet spots - FP but you can always do imperative, compiled but fast compiler, a choice between exceptions and errors as velues etc. It has quite a small userbase and I think it shows. It's not clear what is the best way to get started and there's not that much code out there to study.

Eio will feel familar to anyone who has used Go. Whilst hacking around with it I did keep getting tripped up with flows being closed which turned out to be because I was spinning up a fiber when I should have blocked (like in the callback to run_server).

Hopefully I'll be back soon with a working Raft implementation.

I have been vaguely aware of OCaml for a long time but never dipped my toes in. It has been having a bit of a renaissance recently with multicore support, effects and a more systems focussed spin called OxCaml from Jane Street. Now seems a great time to learn it and I was curious about Eio, a concurrency framework. It uses the aforementioned effects but more interesting to me is it is meant to be deterministic, which could be helpful for testing. Books & Tutorials The first thing to do is pick a tutorial or a book. A quick google search later I had found Real World OCaml. Assuming that it would be similar to Real World Haskell (which I enjoyed and was very hands on) and assuming I already had sufficient FP chops, I dived in. Impatient to look at syntax and semantics I skipped over the more prosey Prologue. If I had read it I would have been told that the book chooses to use Jane Street's Base standard library replacement. It seems that historically the real standard library was quite anemic, only intended to cover the needs of the compiler. This is no longer true and it has slowly been growing. Still, it doesn't seem unreasonable to me that a book called Real World OCaml use the libraries from the biggest real world user of the language. Anyway, I surely learnt my lesson to read things more carefully and went back to the beginning to start again. The first half of the book is well written and I happily followed along. I started to get lost around GADTs and first class modules though. I think the book lacks for exercises, so once something unfamilar comes up you don't have the full grasp of the basics to bail you out. I went searching for something a bit easier and found CS3110 which has both videos and text to suit all tastes. It also had exercises which helped a lot. I would suggest to people that they start with this course and then graduate onto RWO for the more advanced features. I like RWO although if you are looking for a project based book like RWH, you'll be disappointed. How It Feels In The Hand Overall OCaml is very enjoyable to write code in. It has a nice mix of functional and imperative programming. For example, to loop through a list you are either using a recursive function or List.map and friends: let print_recursive list = let rec loop i list = match list with | [] -> () | x :: xs -> Printf.printf "[%d] => %i"; loop (i+1) xs in loop 0 list let print_fn = List.iteri (fun i x -> Printf.printf "[%d] => %i\n" i x) let () = print_recursive ["foo"; "bar"; "baz"]; print_fn ["foo"; "bar"; "baz"]; (* [0] => foo [1] => bar [2] => baz [0] => foo [1] => bar [2] => baz *) but you still have mutable variables and the like if you need them: type client = { mutable request_id : int } let dispatch cli ~msg = let request_id = cli.request_id in cli.request_id <- request_id + 1; (* update request_id *) Printf.printf "Dispatching %d/%s to server\n" request_id msg let () = dispatch_cli ~msg:"Hello"; dispatch_cli ~msg:"World" (* => Dispatching 0/Hello to server => Dispatching 1/World to server *) I will say that the syntax feels quite wordy, e.g. the let .. in, match .. with constructs. I think syntax is the least interesting part of a programming language to me - I was happy even back in the days where Rust had sigils - so it doesn't bother me too much. Having named arguments (with the ~) is very nice. The compiler bothers me more. When it encounters an error it seems to give up on the rest of the file. This makes the iteration loop quite slow - write code, get an error, fix the error, rebuild. There's very little chance to fix errors in bulk, especially if you're focussed on one module. Eio Eio is an effects-based IO concurrency library. It's relatively new but it has some interesting features such as io_uring support. Ultimately my goal is to write an implementation of the Raft consensus algorithm and try test it. The Raft paper says to use remote procedure calls (RPCs) to communicate between the servers. A dumb RPC client/server seems like a great first project. Let's come up with a very simple protocol: a procedure call has a request id (int), a name (string) and a single argument (string). Integers are a single byte and a string is serialised as a byte for the length and then the data. As an example, to call the Echo procedure with Hello as request 10, you would send: 00000000: 0a04 4563 686f 0548 656c 6c6f ..Echo.Hello A response will just be the request id and the string return value. Before we start, we need to familiarise ourselves with some jargon Eio uses for its building blocks: Fiber - a thread of execution. Not a system thread though! Switch - something that groups fibers together. A bit like a sync.WaitGroup in Go Flow - something you can read or write to Stream - a bounded, thread safe queue. Like a Go channel Promise - I'm sure you know this one. Starting with the server we will need to take a Flow and loop, reading procedure calls off it, invoking them and sending a response: open Utils type conn = { src : Eio.Buf_read.t; sink : Eio.Buf_write.t; } let rec conn_loop conn = let request_id = Eio.Buf_read.uint8 conn.src in let procedure_name = read_string conn.src in let body = read_string conn.src in traceln "rx %d/%s(%s)" request_id procedure_name body; (* In a real RPC system we might dispatch the invocation to a worker pool *) let res = match procedure_name with | "Echo" -> body | "Capitalise" -> String.capitalize_ascii body | _ -> failwith "unknown procedure" in traceln "tx %d/%s(%s) => %s" request_id procedure_name body res; Eio.Buf_write.uint8 conn.sink request_id; write_string conn.sink res; conn_loop conn We define a type to hold a connection, as a server can have many of them. Note that we aren't using the flow directly here, instead we are using Buf_read or Buf_write types. That's so we can use buffered reading/writing and be more efficient. Operations on a Flow are done directly on the underlying resource (socket, file etc). Happily these buffered reader/writers have useful helper functions for reading/writing types which we use here to send/receive the request id. read_string/write_string come from Utils: let read_string src = let len = Eio.Buf_read.uint8 src in Eio.Buf_read.take len src let write_string sink s = Eio.Buf_write.uint8 sink (String.length s); Eio.Buf_write.string sink s Next we just need a helper to get the buffered flows and run the loop: let handle_conn flow = let src = Eio.Buf_read.of_flow ~max_size:1024 flow in Eio.Buf_write.with_flow flow @@ fun sink -> conn_loop {src; sink} and a test executable: open Dumb_rpc open Eio.Std let port = 1470 let () = Eio_main.run @@ fun env -> Eio.Switch.run @@ fun sw -> let net = Eio.Stdenv.net env in let handle_client flow addr = traceln "Accepted connection from %a" Eio.Net.Sockaddr.pp addr; Dumb_rpc.Server.handle_conn ~sw flow in let addr = `Tcp (Eio.Net.Ipaddr.V4.loopback, port) in let socket = Eio.Net.listen net ~sw ~reuse_addr:true ~backlog:5 addr in Eio.Net.run_server socket handle_client ~on_error:(traceln "Error handling connection: %a" Fmt.exn) and we're ready to test it: > printf '\x00\x04Echo\x05Hello' | nc localhost 1470 -q0 Hello⏎ > printf '\x01\x0aCapitalise\x07matthew' | nc localhost 1470 -q0 Matthew⏎ Nice! Client For the client let's get a bit more fancy and make it safe to use concurrently. To do that we need to make sure that we are only writing a single procedure call to the socket at any one time. This will be done using two fibers: one for reading and one for writing. The writing one will take its work off a stream that we add to when a method is invoked: type request = { procedure : string; arg : string; } type t = { mutable request_id : int; writeq : request Eio.Stream.t; (* Read right to left: stream of request *) } let rec send_loop cli w = let req = Eio.Stream.take cli.writeq in let request_id = take_and_inc_request_id cli in write_request request; send_loop cli w let invoke cli ~procedure ~arg = let req = {procedure; arg;} in Eio.Stream.add cli.writeq req; But how do we get the response back to the caller? To do that we will send a promise on our stream as well and store it away in a hash table, with the key being the request id. This can be resolved later when the server has responded. Note that in Eio promises are actually split into two halves - the side that waits (Promise.t) and the side that resolves (Promise.u). type waiter = { resolver : string Eio.Promise.u; } type request = { procedure : string; arg : string; resolver : string Eio.Promise.u; } type t = { mutable request_id : int; writeq : request Eio.Stream.t; waiters : (int, waiter) Hashtbl.t; } let rec send_loop cli w = let req = Eio.Stream.take cli.writeq in let request_id = take_and_inc_request_id cli in Hashtbl.add cli.waiters request_id { resolver = req.resolver }; traceln "tx %d/%s(%s)" request_id req.procedure req.arg; write_request request; send_loop cli w let invoke cli ~procedure ~arg = let (promise, resolver) = Eio.Promise.create () in let req = {procedure; arg; resolver; } in Eio.Stream.add cli.writeq req; Eio.Promise.await promise The receive loop can then just read responses and resolve promises: let rec recv_loop cli r = let (request_id, body) = read_response traceln "rx %d => %s" request_id body; let waiter = Hashtbl.find cli.waiters request_id in Promise.resolve waiter.resolver body; recv_loop cli r We can use it like so, from multiple different fibers: open Eio.Std let port = 1470 let () = Eio_main.run @@ fun env -> Eio.Switch.run @@ fun sw -> let net = Eio.Stdenv.net env in let addr = `Tcp (Eio.Net.Ipaddr.V4.loopback, port) in let flow = Eio.Net.connect ~sw net addr in let cli = Dumb_rpc.Client.create () in Eio.Fiber.fork_daemon ~sw (fun () -> Dumb_rpc.Client.run cli ~flow; `Stop_daemon); for i = 0 to 5 do Eio.Fiber.fork ~sw (fun () -> let arg = Printf.sprintf "arg-%d" i in traceln "Invoking Echo(%s)" arg; let res = Dumb_rpc.Client.invoke cli ~procedure:"Echo" ~arg in traceln "Got response Echo(%s) => %s" arg res ) done and see that it works: > ./_build/default/bin/client.exe +Invoking Echo(arg-0) +Invoking Echo(arg-1) +Invoking Echo(arg-2) +Invoking Echo(arg-3) +Invoking Echo(arg-4) +Invoking Echo(arg-5) +tx 0/Echo(arg-0) +tx 1/Echo(arg-1) +tx 2/Echo(arg-2) +tx 3/Echo(arg-3) +rx 0 => arg-0 +Got response Echo(arg-0) => arg-0 +tx 4/Echo(arg-4) +rx 1 => arg-1 +Got response Echo(arg-1) => arg-1 +rx 2 => arg-2 +tx 5/Echo(arg-5) +Got response Echo(arg-2) => arg-2 +rx 3 => arg-3 +rx 4 => arg-4 +Got response Echo(arg-3) => arg-3 +Got response Echo(arg-4) => arg-4 +rx 5 => arg-5 +Got response Echo(arg-5) => arg-5 Concluion Overall OCaml has been fun to learn and easy to use. It sits at a number of sweet spots - FP but you can always do imperative, compiled but fast compiler, a choice between exceptions and errors as velues etc. It has quite a small userbase and I think it shows. It's not clear what is the best way to get started and there's not that much code out there to study. Eio will feel familar to anyone who has used Go. Whilst hacking around with it I did keep getting tripped up with flows being closed which turned out to be because I was spinning up a fiber when I should have blocked (like in the callback to run_server). Hopefully I'll be back soon with a working Raft implementation.

Still, it doesn't seem unreasonable to me that a book called Real World OCaml use the libraries from the biggest real world user of the language.

sentences.en: Impatient to look at syntax and semantics I skipped over the more prosey Prologue.
sentences.zh: 迫不及待地想看语法和语义，我跳过了更散文的序言。

sentences.en: If I had read it I would have been told that the book chooses to use Jane Street's Base standard library replacement.
sentences.zh: 如果我读过它，我会被告知这本书选择使用 Jane Street 的 Base 标准库替换。

sentences.en: It seems that historically the real standard library was quite anemic, only intended to cover the needs of the compiler.
sentences.zh: 从历史上看，真正的标准库似乎相当贫乏，只是为了满足编译器的需求。

sentences.en: This is no longer true and it has slowly been growing.
sentences.zh: 这不再是事实，而且它正在慢慢增长。

sentences.en: Still, it doesn't seem unreasonable to me that a book called Real World OCaml use the libraries from the biggest real world user of the language.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，对我来说，一本名为 Real World OCaml 的书使用该语言最大的现实世界用户的库似乎并不是不合理的。

blocks.html_en: Anyway, I surely learnt my lesson to read things more carefully and went back to the beginning to start again. The first half of the book is well written and I happily followed along. I started to get lost around GADTs and first class modules though. I think the book lacks for exercises, so once something unfamilar comes up you don't have the full grasp of the basics to bail you out.

sentences.en: Anyway, I surely learnt my lesson to read things more carefully and went back to the beginning to start again.
sentences.zh: 不管怎样，我确实吸取了教训，更加仔细地阅读内容，然后回到起点重新开始。

sentences.en: The first half of the book is well written and I happily followed along.
sentences.zh: 这本书的前半部分写得很好，我很高兴地看完了。

sentences.en: I started to get lost around GADTs and first class modules though.
sentences.zh: 不过，我开始迷失在 GADT 和一流模块中。

sentences.en: I think the book lacks for exercises, so once something unfamilar comes up you don't have the full grasp of the basics to bail you out.
sentences.zh: 我认为这本书缺乏练习，所以一旦出现不熟悉的东西，你就无法完全掌握基础知识来摆脱困境。

blocks.html_en: I went searching for something a bit easier and found CS3110 which has both videos and text to suit all tastes. It also had exercises which helped a lot. I would suggest to people that they start with this course and then graduate onto RWO for the more advanced features. I like RWO although if you are looking for a project based book like RWH, you'll be disappointed.

sentences.en: I went searching for something a bit easier and found CS3110 which has both videos and text to suit all tastes.
sentences.zh: 我去寻找一些更简单的东西，发现了 CS3110，它有视频和文本，可以满足所有口味。

sentences.en: It also had exercises which helped a lot.
sentences.zh: 它还提供了很多帮助的练习。

sentences.en: I would suggest to people that they start with this course and then graduate onto RWO for the more advanced features.
sentences.zh: 我建议人们先学习本课程，然后再学习 RWO 以获得更高级的功能。

sentences.en: I like RWO although if you are looking for a project based book like RWH, you'll be disappointed.
sentences.zh: 我喜欢 RWO，但如果您正在寻找像 RWH 这样基于项目的书籍，您会感到失望。

blocks.html_en: How It Feels In The Hand

sentences.en: How It Feels In The Hand
sentences.zh: 拿在手上的感觉如何

blocks.html_en: Overall OCaml is very enjoyable to write code in. It has a nice mix of functional and imperative programming. For example, to loop through a list you are either using a recursive function or List.map and friends:

sentences.en: Overall OCaml is very enjoyable to write code in.
sentences.zh: 总体而言，用 OCaml 编写代码非常令人愉快。

sentences.en: It has a nice mix of functional and imperative programming.
sentences.zh: 它完美地结合了函数式编程和命令式编程。

sentences.en: For example, to loop through a list you are either using a recursive function or List.map and friends:
sentences.zh: 例如，要循环遍历列表，您可以使用递归函数或 List.map 等：

blocks.html_en: but you still have mutable variables and the like if you need them:

sentences.en: but you still have mutable variables and the like if you need them:
sentences.zh: 但如果你需要的话，你仍然有可变变量等：

blocks.html_en: I will say that the syntax feels quite wordy, e.g. the let .. in, match .. with constructs. I think syntax is the least interesting part of a programming language to me - I was happy even back in the days where Rust had sigils - so it doesn't bother me too much. Having named arguments (with the ~) is very nice.

sentences.en: I will say that the syntax feels quite wordy, e.g. the let ..
sentences.zh: 我会说语法感觉很冗长，例如让..

sentences.en: in, match ..
sentences.zh: 中，匹配..

sentences.en: with constructs.
sentences.zh: 与构造。

sentences.en: I think syntax is the least interesting part of a programming language to me - I was happy even back in the days where Rust had sigils - so it doesn't bother me too much.
sentences.zh: 我认为语法对我来说是编程语言中最不有趣的部分 - 即使在 Rust 有标志的日子里我也很高兴 - 所以它并没有太困扰我。

sentences.en: Having named arguments (with the ~) is very nice.
sentences.zh: 命名参数（使用 ~）非常好。

blocks.html_en: The compiler bothers me more. When it encounters an error it seems to give up on the rest of the file. This makes the iteration loop quite slow - write code, get an error, fix the error, rebuild. There's very little chance to fix errors in bulk, especially if you're focussed on one module.

sentences.en: The compiler bothers me more.
sentences.zh: 编译器更让我烦恼。

sentences.en: When it encounters an error it seems to give up on the rest of the file.
sentences.zh: 当它遇到错误时，它似乎放弃了文件的其余部分。

sentences.en: This makes the iteration loop quite slow - write code, get an error, fix the error, rebuild.
sentences.zh: 这使得迭代循环变得非常慢——编写代码，得到错误，修复错误，重建。

sentences.en: There's very little chance to fix errors in bulk, especially if you're focussed on one module.
sentences.zh: 批量修复错误的机会很小，尤其是当您专注于一个模块时。

blocks.html_en: Eio

Eio is an effects-based IO concurrency library. It's relatively new but it has some interesting features such as io_uring support. Ultimately my goal is to write an implementation of the Raft consensus algorithm and try test it.

sentences.en: Eio is an effects-based IO concurrency library.
sentences.zh: Eio 是一个基于效果的 IO 并发库。

sentences.en: It's relatively new but it has some interesting features such as io_uring support.
sentences.zh: 它相对较新，但有一些有趣的功能，例如 io_uring 支持。

sentences.en: Ultimately my goal is to write an implementation of the Raft consensus algorithm and try test it.
sentences.zh: 最终我的目标是编写 Raft 共识算法的实现并尝试测试它。

blocks.html_en: The Raft paper says to use remote procedure calls (RPCs) to communicate between the servers. A dumb RPC client/server seems like a great first project. Let's come up with a very simple protocol: a procedure call has a request id (int), a name (string) and a single argument (string). Integers are a single byte and a string is serialised as a byte for the length and then the data. As an example, to call the Echo procedure with Hello as request 10, you would send:

sentences.en: The Raft paper says to use remote procedure calls (RPCs) to communicate between the servers.
sentences.zh: Raft 论文提出使用远程过程调用（RPC）在服务器之间进行通信。

sentences.en: A dumb RPC client/server seems like a great first project.
sentences.zh: 一个愚蠢的 RPC 客户端/服务器似乎是一个很棒的第一个项目。

sentences.en: Let's come up with a very simple protocol: a procedure call has a request id (int), a name (string) and a single argument (string).
sentences.zh: 让我们想出一个非常简单的协议：过程调用有一个请求 ID（int）、一个名称（字符串）和一个参数（字符串）。

sentences.en: Integers are a single byte and a string is serialised as a byte for the length and then the data.
sentences.zh: 整数是单个字节，字符串被序列化为长度字节，然后是数据。

sentences.en: As an example, to call the Echo procedure with Hello as request 10, you would send:
sentences.zh: 例如，要使用 Hello 作为请求 10 来调用 Echo 过程，您可以发送：

blocks.html_en: A response will just be the request id and the string return value.

sentences.en: A response will just be the request id and the string return value.
sentences.zh: 响应只是请求 ID 和字符串返回值。

blocks.html_en: Before we start, we need to familiarise ourselves with some jargon Eio uses for its building blocks:

sentences.en: Before we start, we need to familiarise ourselves with some jargon Eio uses for its building blocks:
sentences.zh: 在开始之前，我们需要熟悉 Eio 用于其构建块的一些术语：

blocks.html_en: • Fiber - a thread of execution. Not a system thread though!

sentences.en: Fiber - a thread of execution.
sentences.zh: Fiber - 执行线程。

sentences.en: Not a system thread though!
sentences.zh: 但不是系统线程！

blocks.html_en: • Switch - something that groups fibers together. A bit like a sync.WaitGroup in Go

sentences.en: Switch - something that groups fibers together.
sentences.zh: 开关 - 将纤维组合在一起的东西。

sentences.en: A bit like a sync.WaitGroup in Go
sentences.zh: 有点像Go中的sync.WaitGroup

blocks.html_en: • Flow - something you can read or write to

sentences.en: Flow - something you can read or write to
sentences.zh: Flow——你可以读或写的东西

blocks.html_en: • Stream - a bounded, thread safe queue. Like a Go channel

sentences.en: Stream - a bounded, thread safe queue.
sentences.zh: Stream - 有界的、线程安全的队列。

sentences.en: Like a Go channel
sentences.zh: 就像 Go 频道一样

blocks.html_en: • Promise - I'm sure you know this one.

sentences.en: Promise - I'm sure you know this one.
sentences.zh: 保证 - 我相信你知道这个。

blocks.html_en: Starting with the server we will need to take a Flow and loop, reading procedure calls off it, invoking them and sending a response:

sentences.en: Starting with the server we will need to take a Flow and loop, reading procedure calls off it, invoking them and sending a response:
sentences.zh: 从服务器开始，我们需要采用 Flow 和循环，读取过程调用它，调用它们并发送响应：

blocks.html_en: We define a type to hold a connection, as a server can have many of them. Note that we aren't using the flow directly here, instead we are using Buf_read or Buf_write types. That's so we can use buffered reading/writing and be more efficient. Operations on a Flow are done directly on the underlying resource (socket, file etc). Happily these buffered reader/writers have useful helper functions for reading/writing types which we use here to send/receive the request id.

sentences.en: We define a type to hold a connection, as a server can have many of them.
sentences.zh: 我们定义一个类型来保存连接，因为服务器可以有很多连接。

sentences.en: Note that we aren't using the flow directly here, instead we are using Buf_read or Buf_write types.
sentences.zh: 请注意，我们在这里没有直接使用流程，而是使用 Buf_read 或 Buf_write 类型。

sentences.en: That's so we can use buffered reading/writing and be more efficient.
sentences.zh: 这样我们就可以使用缓冲读/写并提高效率。

sentences.en: Operations on a Flow are done directly on the underlying resource (socket, file etc).
sentences.zh: Flow 上的操作直接在底层资源（套接字、文件等）上完成。

sentences.en: Happily these buffered reader/writers have useful helper functions for reading/writing types which we use here to send/receive the request id.
sentences.zh: 令人高兴的是，这些缓冲的读取器/写入器具有用于读取/写入类型的有用辅助函数，我们在这里使用这些函数来发送/接收请求 ID。

blocks.html_en: read_string/write_string come from Utils:

sentences.en: read_string/write_string come from Utils:
sentences.zh: read_string/write_string 来自 Utils：

blocks.html_en: Next we just need a helper to get the buffered flows and run the loop:

sentences.en: Next we just need a helper to get the buffered flows and run the loop:
sentences.zh: 接下来我们只需要一个助手来获取缓冲流并运行循环：

blocks.html_en: and a test executable:

sentences.en: and a test executable:
sentences.zh: 和一个测试可执行文件：

blocks.html_en: and we're ready to test it:

sentences.en: and we're ready to test it:
sentences.zh: 我们准备测试它：

blocks.html_en: Nice!
Client

sentences.en: Client
sentences.zh: 客户

blocks.html_en: For the client let's get a bit more fancy and make it safe to use concurrently. To do that we need to make sure that we are only writing a single procedure call to the socket at any one time. This will be done using two fibers: one for reading and one for writing. The writing one will take its work off a stream that we add to when a method is invoked:

sentences.en: For the client let's get a bit more fancy and make it safe to use concurrently.
sentences.zh: 对于客户端来说，让我们变得更花哨一点，并使其可以安全地同时使用。

sentences.en: To do that we need to make sure that we are only writing a single procedure call to the socket at any one time.
sentences.zh: 为此，我们需要确保在任何时候都只向套接字写入一个过程调用。

sentences.en: This will be done using two fibers: one for reading and one for writing.
sentences.zh: 这将使用两根光纤来完成：一根用于读取，一根用于写入。

sentences.en: The writing one will take its work off a stream that we add to when a method is invoked:
sentences.zh: 写入的工作将从调用方法时添加到的流中取出：

blocks.html_en: But how do we get the response back to the caller? To do that we will send a promise on our stream as well and store it away in a hash table, with the key being the request id. This can be resolved later when the server has responded. Note that in Eio promises are actually split into two halves - the side that waits (Promise.t) and the side that resolves (Promise.u).

sentences.en: But how do we get the response back to the caller?
sentences.zh: 但是我们如何将响应返回给调用者呢？

sentences.en: To do that we will send a promise on our stream as well and store it away in a hash table, with the key being the request id.
sentences.zh: 为此，我们还将在流上发送一个 Promise，并将其存储在哈希表中，键是请求 id。

sentences.en: This can be resolved later when the server has responded.
sentences.zh: 稍后服务器响应后即可解决此问题。

sentences.en: Note that in Eio promises are actually split into two halves - the side that waits (Promise.t) and the side that resolves (Promise.u).
sentences.zh: 请注意，在 Eio 中，promise 实际上分为两半 - 等待的一方 (Promise.t) 和解决的一方 (Promise.u)。

blocks.html_en: The receive loop can then just read responses and resolve promises:

sentences.en: The receive loop can then just read responses and resolve promises:
sentences.zh: 然后，接收循环可以读取响应并解析承诺：

blocks.html_en: We can use it like so, from multiple different fibers:

sentences.en: We can use it like so, from multiple different fibers:
sentences.zh: 我们可以像这样使用它，从多种不同的纤维：

blocks.html_en: and see that it works:

sentences.en: and see that it works:
sentences.zh: 并查看它是否有效：

blocks.html_en: Concluion

sentences.en: Concluion
sentences.zh: 结论

blocks.html_en: Overall OCaml has been fun to learn and easy to use. It sits at a number of sweet spots - FP but you can always do imperative, compiled but fast compiler, a choice between exceptions and errors as velues etc. It has quite a small userbase and I think it shows. It's not clear what is the best way to get started and there's not that much code out there to study.

sentences.en: Overall OCaml has been fun to learn and easy to use.
sentences.zh: 总体而言，OCaml 学起来很有趣并且易于使用。

sentences.en: It sits at a number of sweet spots - FP but you can always do imperative, compiled but fast compiler, a choice between exceptions and errors as velues etc. It has quite a small userbase and I think it shows.
sentences.zh: 它有很多优点 - FP，但你总是可以执行命令式、已编译但快速的编译器、在异常和错误之间进行选择等。它的用户群相当小，我认为它表明了这一点。

sentences.en: It's not clear what is the best way to get started and there's not that much code out there to study.
sentences.zh: 目前尚不清楚什么是最好的入门方法，也没有那么多代码可供研究。

blocks.html_en: Eio will feel familar to anyone who has used Go. Whilst hacking around with it I did keep getting tripped up with flows being closed which turned out to be because I was spinning up a fiber when I should have blocked (like in the callback to run_server).

sentences.en: Eio will feel familar to anyone who has used Go.
sentences.zh: 使用过 Go 的人都会觉得 Eio 很熟悉。

sentences.en: Whilst hacking around with it I did keep getting tripped up with flows being closed which turned out to be because I was spinning up a fiber when I should have blocked (like in the callback to run_server).
sentences.zh: 在使用它时，我确实不断地被关闭的流绊倒，结果是因为我在应该阻塞的时候旋转了光纤（就像在 run_server 的回调中）。

blocks.html_en: Hopefully I'll be back soon with a working Raft implementation.

sentences.en: Hopefully I'll be back soon with a working Raft implementation.
sentences.zh: 希望我很快就能带着一个有效的 Raft 实现回来。

content_html_zh:
我在这个网站上的第一篇博客文章是关于 Haskell 的。 我一直对函数式编程很感兴趣，但 Haskell 的复杂性似乎从来不值得。

我很早以前就对 OCaml 有所了解，但从未尝试过。 最近，它有了一些复兴，包括多核支持、效果和来自 Jane Street 的名为 OxCaml 的更注重系统的旋转。 现在似乎是学习它的好时机，我对 Eio（一个并发框架）很好奇。 它使用上述效果，但对我来说更有趣的是它是确定性的，这可能有助于测试。

书籍和教程

首先要做的就是选择一个教程或一本书。 后来我通过谷歌快速搜索发现了 Real World OCaml。 假设它与现实世界的 Haskell 类似（我喜欢它并且非常实用）并且假设我已经有足够的 FP 能力，我就开始了。

迫不及待地想看语法和语义，我跳过了更散文的序言。 如果我读过它，我会被告知这本书选择使用 Jane Street 的 Base 标准库替换。 从历史上看，真正的标准库似乎相当贫乏，只是为了满足编译器的需求。 这不再是事实，而且它正在慢慢增长。 尽管如此，对我来说，一本名为 Real World OCaml 的书使用该语言最大的现实世界用户的库似乎并不是不合理的。

不管怎样，我确实吸取了教训，更加仔细地阅读内容，然后回到起点重新开始。 这本书的前半部分写得很好，我很高兴地看完了。 不过，我开始迷失在 GADT 和一流模块中。 我认为这本书缺乏练习，所以一旦出现不熟悉的东西，你就无法完全掌握基础知识来摆脱困境。

我去寻找一些更简单的东西，发现了 CS3110，它有视频和文本，可以满足所有口味。 它还提供了很多帮助的练习。 我建议人们先学习本课程，然后再学习 RWO 以获得更高级的功能。 我喜欢 RWO，但如果您正在寻找像 RWH 这样基于项目的书籍，您会感到失望。

拿在手上的感觉如何

总体而言，用 OCaml 编写代码非常令人愉快。 它完美地结合了函数式编程和命令式编程。 例如，要循环遍历列表，您可以使用递归函数或 List.map 等：

let print_recursive list =
  let rec loop i list =
    match list with
    | [] -> ()
    | x :: xs ->
      Printf.printf "[%d] => %i";
      loop (i+1) xs
  in
  loop 0 list

let print_fn = List.iteri (fun i x -> Printf.printf "[%d] => %i\n" i x)

let () =
  print_recursive ["foo"; "bar"; "baz"];
  print_fn ["foo"; "bar"; "baz"];

(*
[0] => foo
[1] => bar
[2] => baz
[0] => foo
[1] => bar
[2] => baz
*)

但如果你需要的话，你仍然有可变变量等：

type client = { mutable request_id : int }

let dispatch cli ~msg =
  let request_id = cli.request_id in
  cli.request_id <- request_id + 1; (* update request_id *)
  
  Printf.printf "Dispatching %d/%s to server\n" request_id msg

let () =
  dispatch_cli ~msg:"Hello";
  dispatch_cli ~msg:"World"

(*
  => Dispatching 0/Hello to server
  => Dispatching 1/World to server
*)

我会说语法感觉很冗长，例如让.. 中，匹配.. 与构造。 我认为语法对我来说是编程语言中最不有趣的部分 - 即使在 Rust 有标志的日子里我也很高兴 - 所以它并没有太困扰我。 命名参数（使用 ~）非常好。

编译器更让我烦恼。 当它遇到错误时，它似乎放弃了文件的其余部分。 这使得迭代循环变得非常慢——编写代码，得到错误，修复错误，重建。 批量修复错误的机会很小，尤其是当您专注于一个模块时。

Eio

Eio 是一个基于效果的 IO 并发库。 它相对较新，但有一些有趣的功能，例如 io_uring 支持。 最终我的目标是编写 Raft 共识算法的实现并尝试测试它。

Raft 论文提出使用远程过程调用（RPC）在服务器之间进行通信。 一个愚蠢的 RPC 客户端/服务器似乎是一个很棒的第一个项目。 让我们想出一个非常简单的协议：过程调用有一个请求 ID（int）、一个名称（字符串）和一个参数（字符串）。 整数是单个字节，字符串被序列化为长度字节，然后是数据。 例如，要使用 Hello 作为请求 10 来调用 Echo 过程，您可以发送：

00000000: 0a04 4563 686f 0548 656c 6c6f            ..Echo.Hello

响应只是请求 ID 和字符串返回值。

在开始之前，我们需要熟悉 Eio 用于其构建块的一些术语：

  • Fiber - 执行线程。 但不是系统线程！
  • 开关 - 将纤维组合在一起的东西。 有点像Go中的sync.WaitGroup
  • Flow——你可以读或写的东西
  • Stream - 有界的、线程安全的队列。 就像 Go 频道一样
  • 保证 - 我相信你知道这个。

从服务器开始，我们需要采用 Flow 和循环，读取过程调用它，调用它们并发送响应：

open Utils

type conn = {
  src : Eio.Buf_read.t;
  sink : Eio.Buf_write.t;
}

let rec conn_loop conn =
  let request_id = Eio.Buf_read.uint8 conn.src in

  let procedure_name = read_string conn.src in
  let body = read_string conn.src in
  
  traceln "rx %d/%s(%s)" request_id procedure_name body;

  (* In a real RPC system we might dispatch the invocation to a worker pool *)
  let res = match procedure_name with
  | "Echo" -> body
  | "Capitalise" -> String.capitalize_ascii body
  | _ -> failwith "unknown procedure"
  in

  traceln "tx %d/%s(%s) => %s" request_id procedure_name body res;

  Eio.Buf_write.uint8 conn.sink request_id;
  write_string conn.sink res;

  conn_loop conn

我们定义一个类型来保存连接，因为服务器可以有很多连接。 请注意，我们在这里没有直接使用流程，而是使用 Buf_read 或 Buf_write 类型。 这样我们就可以使用缓冲读/写并提高效率。 Flow 上的操作直接在底层资源（套接字、文件等）上完成。 令人高兴的是，这些缓冲的读取器/写入器具有用于读取/写入类型的有用辅助函数，我们在这里使用这些函数来发送/接收请求 ID。

read_string/write_string 来自 Utils：

let read_string src =
  let len = Eio.Buf_read.uint8 src in
  Eio.Buf_read.take len src
  
let write_string sink s =
  Eio.Buf_write.uint8 sink (String.length s);
  Eio.Buf_write.string sink s

接下来我们只需要一个助手来获取缓冲流并运行循环：

let handle_conn flow =
  let src = Eio.Buf_read.of_flow ~max_size:1024 flow in
  Eio.Buf_write.with_flow flow @@ fun sink ->
  conn_loop {src; sink}

和一个测试可执行文件：

open Dumb_rpc
open Eio.Std

let port = 1470

let () =
  Eio_main.run @@ fun env ->
  Eio.Switch.run @@ fun sw ->
  let net = Eio.Stdenv.net env in

  let handle_client flow addr =
    traceln "Accepted connection from %a" Eio.Net.Sockaddr.pp addr;
    Dumb_rpc.Server.handle_conn ~sw flow
  in

  let addr = `Tcp (Eio.Net.Ipaddr.V4.loopback, port) in
  let socket = Eio.Net.listen net ~sw ~reuse_addr:true ~backlog:5 addr in
  Eio.Net.run_server socket handle_client
    ~on_error:(traceln "Error handling connection: %a" Fmt.exn)

我们准备测试它：

> printf '\x00\x04Echo\x05Hello' | nc localhost 1470 -q0
Hello⏎
> printf '\x01\x0aCapitalise\x07matthew' | nc localhost 1470 -q0
Matthew⏎

Nice!

客户

对于客户端来说，让我们变得更花哨一点，并使其可以安全地同时使用。 为此，我们需要确保在任何时候都只向套接字写入一个过程调用。 这将使用两根光纤来完成：一根用于读取，一根用于写入。 写入的工作将从调用方法时添加到的流中取出：

type request = { procedure : string; arg : string; }

type t = {
  mutable request_id : int;
  writeq : request Eio.Stream.t; (* Read right to left: stream of request *)
}

let rec send_loop cli w =
  let req = Eio.Stream.take cli.writeq in
  let request_id = take_and_inc_request_id cli in
  write_request request;
  send_loop cli w

let invoke cli ~procedure ~arg =
  let req = {procedure; arg;} in
  Eio.Stream.add cli.writeq req;

但是我们如何将响应返回给调用者呢？ 为此，我们还将在流上发送一个 Promise，并将其存储在哈希表中，键是请求 id。 稍后服务器响应后即可解决此问题。 请注意，在 Eio 中，promise 实际上分为两半 - 等待的一方 (Promise.t) 和解决的一方 (Promise.u)。

type waiter = { resolver : string Eio.Promise.u; }

type request = {
  procedure : string;
  arg : string;
  resolver : string Eio.Promise.u;
}

type t = {
  mutable request_id : int;

  writeq : request Eio.Stream.t;
  waiters : (int, waiter) Hashtbl.t;
}

let rec send_loop cli w =
  let req = Eio.Stream.take cli.writeq in
  let request_id = take_and_inc_request_id cli in
  Hashtbl.add cli.waiters request_id { resolver = req.resolver };
  
  traceln "tx %d/%s(%s)" request_id req.procedure req.arg;
  write_request request;

  send_loop cli w

let invoke cli ~procedure ~arg =
  let (promise, resolver) = Eio.Promise.create () in
  let req = {procedure; arg; resolver; } in
  Eio.Stream.add cli.writeq req;

  Eio.Promise.await promise

然后，接收循环可以读取响应并解析承诺：

let rec recv_loop cli r =
  let (request_id, body) = read_response
  
  traceln "rx %d => %s" request_id body;
  let waiter = Hashtbl.find cli.waiters request_id in
  Promise.resolve waiter.resolver body;
  
  recv_loop cli r

我们可以像这样使用它，从多种不同的纤维：

open Eio.Std

let port = 1470

let () =
  Eio_main.run @@ fun env ->
  Eio.Switch.run @@ fun sw ->
  let net = Eio.Stdenv.net env in

  let addr = `Tcp (Eio.Net.Ipaddr.V4.loopback, port) in
  let flow = Eio.Net.connect ~sw net addr in

  let cli = Dumb_rpc.Client.create () in
  Eio.Fiber.fork_daemon ~sw (fun () -> Dumb_rpc.Client.run cli ~flow; `Stop_daemon);
  
  for i = 0 to 5 do
    Eio.Fiber.fork ~sw (fun () ->
      let arg = Printf.sprintf "arg-%d" i in
      traceln "Invoking Echo(%s)" arg;
      let res = Dumb_rpc.Client.invoke cli ~procedure:"Echo" ~arg in
      traceln "Got response Echo(%s) => %s" arg res
    )
  done

并查看它是否有效：

> ./_build/default/bin/client.exe
+Invoking Echo(arg-0)
+Invoking Echo(arg-1)
+Invoking Echo(arg-2)
+Invoking Echo(arg-3)
+Invoking Echo(arg-4)
+Invoking Echo(arg-5)
+tx 0/Echo(arg-0)
+tx 1/Echo(arg-1)
+tx 2/Echo(arg-2)
+tx 3/Echo(arg-3)
+rx 0 => arg-0
+Got response Echo(arg-0) => arg-0
+tx 4/Echo(arg-4)
+rx 1 => arg-1
+Got response Echo(arg-1) => arg-1
+rx 2 => arg-2
+tx 5/Echo(arg-5)
+Got response Echo(arg-2) => arg-2
+rx 3 => arg-3
+rx 4 => arg-4
+Got response Echo(arg-3) => arg-3
+Got response Echo(arg-4) => arg-4
+rx 5 => arg-5
+Got response Echo(arg-5) => arg-5

结论

总体而言，OCaml 学起来很有趣并且易于使用。 它有很多优点 - FP，但你总是可以执行命令式、已编译但快速的编译器、在异常和错误之间进行选择等。它的用户群相当小，我认为它表明了这一点。 目前尚不清楚什么是最好的入门方法，也没有那么多代码可供研究。

使用过 Go 的人都会觉得 Eio 很熟悉。 在使用它时，我确实不断地被关闭的流绊倒，结果是因为我在应该阻塞的时候旋转了光纤（就像在 run_server 的回调中）。

希望我很快就能带着一个有效的 Raft 实现回来。
